title: practical english usage - Part 82
content:
Why won't you let me explain? I heard her open the door and go out.

followed by object + infinitive without to (►  91).

Let, make, see, hear, feel, watch, notice, have, and sometimes know and help, are expect     like       remind encourage   leave      recommend compel      invite      prefer      wish (also ►    632) command    intend     persuade    warn cause      instruct     permit      want beg        help (also ►    483)     order      trouble (can't) bear   hate       oblige      tempt ask       get (also ►     108)        need       tell allow      force       mean       teach advise     forbid     love       request Some common verbs that can be followed by object + infinitive: Section Infinitives,      -ing    forms     and   Past   Participles       after    Nouns,    Verbs,   etc         9 grammar •  100   -ing forms after verbs: I enjoy travelling.

object + -ing form, especially in a formal style. (►   94.3 for details.) Note that after many verbs we  can use possessive + -ing form rather than Try to stop/prevent them (from) finding out.

-ing form.

Stop (in an informal style) and prevent are often followed by object + from + We found a dead mouse lying on the kitchen floor.

I caught the next-door children stealing my apples.

He spends all his time gardening.

Nobody can stop him  doing what he wants to.

I can't imagine him  working in an office.

/ dislike people telling me what to think.

object + -ing form.

Some of the verbs listed above, and some others, can be followed by 2 verb    + object     + -ing    form:   I dislike       people        telling       me    . . .

a particular verb. It is best to check  in a good dictionary.

Unfortunately, there is no easy way to decide which  structures are possible after Some verbs can be followed by both -ing forms and infinitives (►   100.4 below).

detest     forgive     postpone deny      finish      miss delay      feel like     mind       understand contemplate fancy      mention     suggest consider    face       leave off     (can't) stand laughing)    excuse      keep (on)     risk burst out (crying/escape     involve     resist avoid      enjoy       imagine     resent appreciate  endure     (can't) help    put off admit      dislike     give up      practise Some common verbs that are normally followed by -ing forms: not The doctor suggested taking a long rest, (     The doctor suggested-to-take-...) not She's given up smoking, (     ... given -op to smoke.) not He's finished repairing the car. (     He's finished to repair-...) not / enjoy travelling, (     I enjoy to travel:) an infinitive.

After some verbs we  can use an -ing form (gerund), but not normally 1  verbs     that    can   be   followed       by   -ing    forms 100 -ing    forms     after    verbs:   I enjoy      travelling.

Let's go see if Anne's home. Go jump  in the river. Come sit on my lap.

and come.

In informal American English, and is sometimes dropped after the base forms go 3 American      English grammar *101  infinitives after adjectives: pleased to see you an identifying relative clause (►   234).

Superlatives can be followed by an infinitive structure. The  meaning is similar to 3 superlatives,         etc:  the    oldest       athlete        to   win    ...

For structures with preparatory it (e.g. It is important to get enough sleep). ►     268.

You're welcome to stay as long as you like.

She's certain to win.      You were lucky not to be killed.

I was stupid to believe him. It's very likely to rain.

We were right to start early. Be careful not to wake the children.

welcome, careful, due, fit, able (►  85), likely (►  516), lucky.

be followed by infinitives. Examples: right, wrong, stupid, certain (►   105.15), Besides adjectives referring to reactions and feelings, many other adjectives can 2 other     adjectives:      certain        to   win For structures with for (e.g. She's anxious for the children to go home), ►     113.

with a difference of meaning: for details, ►     105.13-105.16.

(e.g. afraid, sure) can be followed by either an infinitive or an -ing form, often preposition + -ing form (►    103), or by that-dauses (►   192). Some adjectives Not all adjectives of this kind are followed by infinitives. Some are followed by Most people are afraid to hear the truth about themselves.
